questions.questionText: The bone that forms the keystone of the cranial vault is the:
questions.explanation: The sphenoid bone is called the 'keystone of the skull' because it articulates with all other cranial bones (frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital, ethmoid, and even zygomatic and palatine). It houses the pituitary gland in the sella turcica (hypophyseal fossa). Its greater wing forms part of the middle cranial fossa. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Skull Bones.

questions.questionText: The weakest part of the skull is the:
questions.explanation: Pterion (junction of frontal, parietal, temporal, and greater wing of sphenoid) is the thinnest and weakest area of the skull. Trauma here damages the anterior branch of the middle meningeal artery → epidural hematoma. It lies 2 finger breadths above the zygomatic arch and 1 thumb breadth behind the frontal process of the zygomatic bone. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Skull Lateral View.

questions.questionText: The atlas (C1 vertebra) differs from other cervical vertebrae because it:
questions.optionB: Lacks a body and spinous process
questions.explanation: Atlas (C1) is unique: no body (replaced by the anterior arch) and no spinous process (has a posterior tubercle instead). It supports the skull and allows nodding (yes movement) at the atlanto-occipital joint. The axis (C2) has the dens (odontoid process), which acts as a pivot for atlas rotation (head turning/no movement at atlanto-axial joint). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cervical Vertebrae.

questions.questionText: The fontanelle that closes last in an infant is the:
questions.explanation: Anterior fontanelle (bregma): largest, diamond-shaped, at junction of sagittal and coronal sutures. Closes at 18 months (range 12-24 months). Posterior fontanelle (lambda): triangular, closes at 2-3 months. Early closure → craniosynostosis. Delayed closure → rickets, hypothyroidism, Down syndrome, hydrocephalus. Bulging fontanelle → raised ICP. Depressed → dehydration. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Neonatal Skull.

questions.questionText: The bone that is most commonly fractured in the body is the:
questions.explanation: The clavicle is the most commonly fractured bone, usually at the junction of the middle and lateral thirds (weakest point). Mechanism: FOOSH (fall on outstretched hand) or direct trauma. The medial fragment is pulled upward by sternocleidomastoid, and the lateral fragment drops due to gravity and pectoralis major pull. Subclavian vessels and brachial plexus may be damaged. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Clavicle.

questions.questionText: The type of ossification seen in the clavicle is:
questions.explanation: The clavicle is unique — it is the first bone to ossify (5th week of fetal life) and is the last to fuse its epiphysis (medial end, 25 years). It ossifies by BOTH intramembranous (most of the shaft — membranous ossification) and endochondral (medial end) ossification. It is also the only long bone that ossifies in membrane. Most other long bones ossify by endochondral ossification. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Clavicle Development.

questions.questionText: The vertebral level of the highest point of the iliac crest is:
questions.explanation: The highest point of the iliac crest corresponds to L4 vertebral level (supracristal plane). This landmark is used for lumbar puncture — a line connecting the highest points of both iliac crests passes through the L4 spinous process (or L4-L5 interspace). The iliac crest also provides bone for bone grafting (iliac crest bone graft — ICBG). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Surface Anatomy of Back.

questions.questionText: Sesamoid bones develop within:
questions.explanation: Sesamoid bones develop within tendons, usually where tendons cross joints. The largest sesamoid bone is the patella (within the quadriceps tendon). Other examples: sesamoid bones in the tendons of flexor hallucis brevis (under first metatarsal head), pisiform (within tendon of FCU). The fabella is an occasional sesamoid in the lateral head of gastrocnemius. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Sesamoid Bones.

questions.questionText: The true ribs (vertebrosternal ribs) are:
questions.explanation: True ribs (1-7): attach directly to the sternum via their own costal cartilage. False ribs (8-10/vertebrochondral): attach to sternum indirectly through the cartilage of the rib above. Floating ribs (11-12): have no anterior attachment. The 1st rib is the shortest, broadest, and most curved. The 12th rib is the shortest rib. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Thoracic Cage.

questions.questionText: The odontoid process (dens) is a feature of:
questions.explanation: The dens (odontoid process) is the superior projection of the body of the axis (C2). It represents the body of the atlas that fused with C2 during development. It serves as a pivot for rotation of the atlas and skull. Dens fractures are classified as Type I, II (most common and poorly healing), and III. The transverse ligament of the atlas holds the dens in place. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Axis Vertebra.

questions.questionText: A cervical rib can compress the:
questions.optionB: Lower trunk of brachial plexus and subclavian artery
questions.explanation: A cervical rib (arising from C7) can compress the lower trunk of the brachial plexus (C8-T1) and subclavian artery → thoracic outlet syndrome. Symptoms: hand weakness (intrinsic muscles — C8-T1 territory), numbness in medial forearm and hand, vascular symptoms (pallor, cold hand, absent radial pulse on arm elevation). Adson's test, Roos test are used for diagnosis. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Thoracic Outlet.

questions.questionText: The sacrum is formed by the fusion of how many vertebrae?
questions.explanation: The sacrum is formed by the fusion of 5 sacral vertebrae (S1-S5). The sacral promontory (anterior border of S1) is an important obstetric landmark — the anteroposterior diameter from the promontory to the symphysis pubis is the 'true conjugate' (~10 cm). The sacral hiatus (at S4-S5) is used for caudal epidural anesthesia. The sacral canal contains the cauda equina and filum terminale. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Sacrum.

questions.questionText: The growth plate (epiphyseal plate) that contributes most to the length of the femur is at the:
questions.explanation: In the femur, the lower (distal) epiphysis contributes more to longitudinal growth. General rule: 'growth towards the knee, away from the elbow' — the epiphyseal plates near the knee (lower femur, upper tibia) and away from the elbow (upper humerus, lower radius) are the more active growth plates. This is why injuries near the knee in children can cause significant growth disturbance. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Bone Growth.

questions.questionText: The foramen magnum transmits:
questions.optionB: Spinal cord (medulla-spinal junction), vertebral arteries, spinal accessory nerve
questions.explanation: Foramen magnum transmits: medulla oblongata/spinal cord junction, vertebral arteries, anterior and posterior spinal arteries, spinal accessory nerve (ascending cranial root), meninges, and apical ligament of dens. Tonsillar herniation through the foramen magnum ('coning') is a fatal consequence of raised ICP — cerebellar tonsils compress the medulla. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Skull Base.

questions.questionText: Endochondral ossification occurs in all the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Skull vault bones (parietal, frontal, squamous temporal, and squamous occipital) develop by intramembranous ossification (mesenchyme → bone directly, no cartilage intermediate). Endochondral ossification (cartilage model → bone replacement) occurs in: long bones, vertebral bodies, skull base bones. The skull base (chondrocranium) undergoes endochondral ossification. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Bone Development.

questions.questionText: The cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone transmits:
questions.explanation: The cribriform plate (horizontal plate of ethmoid) has multiple small foramina through which olfactory nerve fibers (CN I) pass from the nasal mucosa to the olfactory bulb. Anterior ethmoidal artery and nerve also pass through. CSF rhinorrhea can occur with cribriform plate fracture (anterior cranial fossa fracture). Esthesioneuroblastoma (olfactory neuroblastoma) arises from the olfactory epithelium here. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Anterior Cranial Fossa.

questions.questionText: The bone not forming part of the orbit is:
questions.explanation: Seven bones form the orbit (mnemonic: 'My Fat Zebra Slowly Eats Lots of Pears'): Maxilla, Frontal, Zygomatic, Sphenoid (greater and lesser wings), Ethmoid, Lacrimal, Palatine. The TEMPORAL bone does NOT contribute to the orbit. The weakest wall is the floor (maxilla — blow-out fracture from blunt trauma → entrapment of inferior rectus → diplopia on upward gaze). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Orbit.

questions.questionText: The typical cervical vertebra is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Transverse foramina, bifid spinous process, triangular vertebral foramen
questions.optionC: Superior and inferior articular facets facing anteroposteriorly
questions.explanation: Typical cervical vertebrae (C3-C6) have: transverse foramina (transmitting vertebral artery — except C7), bifid spinous process, triangular vertebral foramen, small body. Atypical: C1 (atlas — no body), C2 (axis — dens), C7 (vertebra prominens — longest non-bifid spine, no vertebral artery through foramen). Thoracic: costal facets, circular foramen. Lumbar: kidney-shaped body, largest. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Vertebral Column.

questions.questionText: The first bone to ossify in the human body is the:
questions.explanation: The clavicle is the first bone to begin ossification (5th-6th week of fetal life, by intramembranous ossification). It is also the LAST bone to complete ossification (medial epiphysis fuses at 25 years). The mandible is often cited as the first bone to start ossifying in the face. The clavicle's early ossification and late fusion make it clinically significant. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Clavicle Development.

questions.questionText: The supracondylar process of the humerus, when present, can compress which nerve?
questions.explanation: The supracondylar process is an occasional bony spur ~5 cm above the medial epicondyle. When present, a fibrous band (ligament of Struthers) connects it to the medial epicondyle, creating a tunnel through which the median nerve and brachial artery pass. This can cause compression — supracondylar process syndrome (forearm pain, median nerve symptoms). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Humerus Variants.

questions.questionText: The bone that forms the posterior part of the hard palate is:
questions.explanation: The hard palate is formed by the palatine process of maxilla (anterior 2/3) and the horizontal plate of the palatine bone (posterior 1/3). The greater palatine foramen (transmitting greater palatine nerve and vessels) is located at the posterolateral corner. The incisive foramen is anterior (transmitting nasopalatine nerve). Greater palatine nerve block is used for palatal anesthesia. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Palate.

questions.questionText: The largest tarsal bone is the:
questions.explanation: Calcaneus (os calcis) is the largest tarsal bone. It sustains the body weight during standing. Calcaneal fractures occur from falls (landing on heels) — associated with burst fractures of the thoracolumbar spine (10% association). Boehler's angle (<20°) indicates calcaneal fracture. It has the sustentaculum tali which supports the head of talus and has the flexor hallucis longus tendon grooved beneath it. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Foot Bones.

questions.questionText: The primary center of ossification appears FIRST in which bone of the wrist?
questions.explanation: Carpal bones ossify postnatally. The capitate is the first to ossify (~1-3 months), followed by the hamate. The order of carpal bone ossification: Capitate → Hamate → Triquetral → Lunate → Scaphoid → Trapezium → Trapezoid → Pisiform (last, ~9-12 years). This is clinically important for assessing bone age in children. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Carpal Bone Development.

questions.questionText: The angle of inclination (neck-shaft angle) of the femur in adults is approximately:
questions.explanation: The normal neck-shaft angle (angle of inclination) of the femur is ~125° in adults (range 120-135°). Increased angle = coxa valga (>135°). Decreased angle = coxa vara (<120°). In infants, the angle is ~150° and gradually decreases with age. Coxa vara causes apparent shortening, and the Trendelenburg gait. Coxa valga is associated with DDH. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Femur.

questions.questionText: The interosseous membrane of the forearm transfers forces from the:
questions.explanation: The interosseous membrane of the forearm transfers forces from the radius to the ulna (and then to the humerus at the elbow). This is important because the weight-bearing wrist joint is mainly between the radius and the carpal bones (scaphoid and lunate). Forces from FOOSH travel: hand → radius → interosseous membrane → ulna → humerus. The membrane also increases surface area for muscle attachment. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Forearm Interosseous Membrane.

questions.questionText: The weight-bearing line of the lower limb passes through:
questions.optionA: Center of the hip, lateral to knee, lateral to ankle
questions.optionB: Center of hip, center of knee, center of ankle
questions.optionC: Medial to hip, center of knee, medial to ankle
questions.optionD: Center of hip, medial to knee, lateral to ankle
questions.explanation: The mechanical axis (weight-bearing line) of the lower limb runs from the center of the femoral head through the center of the knee to the center of the ankle joint. The anatomical axis of the femur is slightly different due to the neck-shaft angle. Genu valgum (knock-knees): mechanical axis falls lateral to the knee. Genu varum (bow-legs): mechanical axis falls medial. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Lower Limb Alignment.

questions.questionText: The ossification center that appears at birth in the lower end of the femur is used as a sign of:
questions.explanation: The ossification center at the lower end of the femur (distal femoral epiphysis) appears around 36 weeks of gestation (9th month) and is present at birth in full-term infants. Its presence on X-ray indicates full-term maturity — used in forensic medicine for determining age at birth. The upper end of the tibia ossification center appears shortly after birth. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Forensic Osteology.

questions.questionText: The manubrium articulates with the body of the sternum forming a:
questions.explanation: The manubriosternal joint is a secondary cartilaginous joint (symphysis) — joined by fibrocartilage. It allows a small amount of movement during breathing. The sternal angle (of Louis) at this joint is a key surface landmark (T4 level). The xiphisternal joint is a primary cartilaginous joint (synchondrosis). Other symphyses: pubic symphysis, intervertebral disc joints. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Joints of Thorax.
